content:
Matrices arose from the need to organize and solve large systems of linear equations systematically. Instead of writing each equation separately, mathematicians grouped coefficients into arrays to perform operations on whole systems at once.

During the 19th century, work by Cayley, Sylvester and others formalized matrices as algebraic objects with their own operations, independent of specific equations. This abstraction made it possible to study properties like determinants, inverses and eigenvalues in a unified framework.

In the 20th century, matrices became central in numerical linear algebra, physics, engineering and later computer science and machine learning. Efficient algorithms and hardware-friendly representations made matrix operations the core building blocks of modern scientific computing.

timeline.label: Early linear systems
timeline.description: Matrices implicitly used to tabulate coefficients of simultaneous linear equations, especially in astronomy and engineering problems.

timeline.label: 19th century formalization
timeline.description: Cayley and others define matrices and matrix multiplication explicitly, establishing matrix algebra as a standalone subject.

timeline.label: Modern numerical linear algebra
timeline.description: Development of efficient algorithms and libraries makes large-scale matrix computation practical, powering optimization and machine learning.
